did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning and computer vision techniques for melanoma lesion detection. Specifically, it employs normalized cross-correlation-based k-means clustering for segmentation, Hu Moments and GLCM-based Haralick features for feature extraction, and multiple classifiers including Random Forest, Decision Tree, SVM, Gaussian Naïve Bayes, Logistic Regression, and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) for classification of benign vs. malignant lesions using ISIC datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing a new segmentation model using normalized cross-correlation-based k-means clustering for melanoma detection. It details specific methods (Hu Moments, GLCM-based Haralick features), datasets used (ISIC 2017, 2019, 2020), and reports original results including accuracy metrics (e.g., 99.46% for Random Forest) and comparisons with classifiers like SVM, KNN, and Logistic Regression.

title: Improved segmentation model for melanoma lesion detection using normalized cross-correlation-based k-means clustering
brief_summary: This study proposes an improved segmentation model for melanoma lesion detection using normalized cross-correlation-based k-means clustering to identify regions of interest from dermoscopic images. The system extracts shape features via Hu Moments and textural features using GLCM-based Haralick descriptors from the segmented lesions. Classification into benign or malignant categories is performed using multiple machine learning algorithms, including Random Forest, Decision Tree, SVM, Gaussian Naïve Bayes, Logistic Regression, and KNN. The model was evaluated on the ISIC 2017, 2019, and 2020 datasets. The Random Forest classifier achieved the highest accuracy of 99.46% on the ISIC-2017 dataset, while KNN and SVM attained 99.29% and 99.38% on ISIC-2019 and ISIC-2020, respectively. The authors conclude that the proposed method is more robust and accurate than existing techniques while incorporating extensive feature information.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on detecting and classifying melanoma lesions. The classification task distinguishes between benign and malignant (cancerous) melanoma cases using ISIC 2017, 2019, and 2020 datasets.
Used as the negative class for binary classification against malignant melanoma. The paper mentions classifying skin lesions as 'cancerous or non-cancerous' and 'benign or malignant'.

all_task_types.target: Benign vs. Malignant melanoma lesion categories
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to identify skin lesions as 'cancerous or non-cancerous' (benign or malignant). It uses multiple classifiers (Random Forest, Decision Tree, SVM, Gaussian Naïve Bayes, Logistic Regression, KNN) on features extracted from segmented lesions. The primary task is classification, with segmentation serving as a preprocessing step for feature extraction. Accuracy metrics (e.g., 99.46% for Random Forest on ISIC-2017) are reported for the classification task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopic image dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) used for melanoma detection.

use.training: Used for training the classification models (Random Forest, Decision Tree, SVM, Gaussian Naïve Bayes, Logistic Regression, KNN) after segmentation and feature extraction.
use.testing: Used for testing; Random Forest achieved 99.46% accuracy on this dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopic image dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) containing 25,331 images with eight different types of melanoma diseases.

use.training: Used for training the classification models.
use.testing: Used for testing; KNN achieved 99.29% accuracy on this dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopic image dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) used for melanoma detection.

use.training: Used for training the classification models.
use.testing: Used for testing; SVM achieved 99.38% accuracy on this dataset.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the class separability and reliability of the studied models by plotting True Positive Rate (TPR) against False Positive Rate (FPR) at different thresholds.
To evaluate classification performance by calculating True Positives (TP), True Negatives (TN), False Positives (FP), and False Negatives (FN) for each classifier.
Used within the Random Forest classifier to create a bootstrapped sample of the training data for building individual trees.
Used in the Random Forest classifier to assign an unknown pixel to a class after trees are built.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC curves without statistical significance testing.

ISIC-2017.notes: Random Forest achieved the highest accuracy on this dataset. The dataset was divided into 70% training and 30% testing images.

ISIC-2019.notes: KNN achieved the highest accuracy on this dataset.

ISIC-2020.notes: SVM achieved the highest accuracy on this dataset.

architecture_used.main_model: Normalized cross-correlation-based k-means clustering model for segmentation, followed by Hu Moments and GLCM-based Haralick feature extraction, and multiple classical machine learning classifiers (Random Forest, Decision Tree, SVM, Gaussian Naïve Bayes, Logistic Regression, K-Nearest Neighbor).
architecture_used.pretraining: None reported; the system uses traditional machine learning and image processing techniques rather than deep learning models.
architecture_used.input_size: Template image resized to 100x100 pixels; dataset images converted from RGB to Grayscale.

architecture_details.segmentation_method: Normalized cross-correlation-based template matching to dynamically calculate the number of clusters (k) for k-means clustering.
feature_extraction: Hu Moments for shape classification and part recognition.
GLCM-based Haralick feature extractor for textural features.

hyperparameters.k_means_clusters: Dynamically calculated based on the number of matched areas found via normalized cross-correlation.
hyperparameters.knn_neighbors: Not explicitly specified, described as 'typically small'.

classification_or_output_head.method: Unweighted voting for Random Forest; majority vote for KNN; maximum-margin hyperplane for SVM; probabilistic output for Logistic Regression and Naïve Bayes.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Interpretable deep learning and ensemble stacking of ML models
preprocessing_techniques: Template matching using normalized cross-correlation to identify the region of interest (ROI) and dynamically determine the number of clusters for segmentation.
Conversion of RGB dermoscopic images to grayscale to reduce computation requirements and enhance visualization.
Histogram equalization applied to the segmented output image for contrast enhancement.
Resizing of the template image to 100x100 pixels.
conclusions: The proposed normalized cross-correlation-based k-means clustering segmentation model is more robust and accurate than existing methods and incorporates more feature information from images.

The method dynamically calculates the number of clusters for k-means clustering using template matching, avoiding the need for manual input of cluster numbers which can affect results on large datasets.
The Random Forest classifier achieved the highest accuracy of 99.46% on the ISIC-2017 dataset.
The K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) classifier achieved the highest accuracy of 99.29% on the ISIC-2019 dataset.
The Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier achieved the highest accuracy of 99.38% on the ISIC-2020 dataset.
The proposed system effectively detects and classifies melanoma lesions as benign or malignant using features extracted via Hu Moments (shape) and GLCM-based Haralick descriptors (texture).
The proposed approach outperforms state-of-the-art algorithms with high accuracy and low computational cost.
The system utilizes traditional machine learning and image processing techniques rather than deep learning for the core segmentation and classification pipeline.
important_remarks: The paper is an unedited author's version accepted for publication in IEEE Access; content may change prior to final publication.
The study relies on publicly available ISIC 2017, 2019, and 2020 datasets without reporting specific train/validation/test splits or class counts, limiting reproducibility.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported; performance claims are based solely on accuracy metrics.
The dataset split is reported as 70% training and 30% testing, but the methodology for cross-validation or independent external validation is not detailed.

The authors claim the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art deep learning models, but specific comparative baselines and their corresponding metrics are not fully detailed in the provided context.
The study uses traditional machine learning (Random Forest, SVM, KNN, etc.) rather than deep learning, which may limit feature representation complexity compared to modern CNNs.
No data leakage risks are explicitly addressed, though the use of public datasets reduces this risk; however, the lack of detailed preprocessing pipelines for each dataset split is a concern.

The model's robustness is claimed based on dynamic cluster calculation via normalized cross-correlation, but this is not validated against varying image qualities or lighting conditions beyond the ISIC datasets.
The authors declare no financial assistance and no conflict of interests, which supports the independence of the research.
The study focuses on binary classification (benign vs. malignant), which may not reflect the complexity of real-world dermatological diagnosis involving multiple lesion types.
